summary: This excerpt from the Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy outlines Heidegger's categorization of the 'kinds of being', specifically detailing 'availableness' (readiness-to-hand) as it applies to tools and equipment. It also highlights his lifelong anti-individualism regarding meaning, tracing how his concept of the social disclosure of being evolved from the 'anyone-self' to the 'Volk' and later to localities of mortals.

principles.statement: The primary disclosure of being is social in character, not individual.
principles.rationale: The way we encounter entities and interpret the world is prescribed by a collective entity, such as the 'anyone-self', rather than isolated individual minds.
principles.source_quote: the anyone-self... articulates the referential context of significance

mental_models.name: Kinds of Being
mental_models.description: A lens for understanding entities based on how they exist in relation to human activity, categorized primarily into availableness (readiness-to-hand), occurrentness (presence-at-hand), and existence (Dasein).
mental_models.example: Works of art, life, numbers, and nature may also have distinct kinds of being.

mental_models.name: Availableness (Readiness-to-Hand)
mental_models.description: A specific kind of being where an entity is encountered as usable or serviceable for some activity or task.
mental_models.example: Tools and instruments—broadly speaking, equipment—are paradigmatic examples of entities that have availableness as their kind of being.

mental_models.name: The Anyone-Self
mental_models.description: The social construct or collective norm that dictates how individuals interpret the world and encounter other entities.

signature_quotes.text: articulates the referential context of significance
signature_quotes.context: Describing the role of the 'anyone-self' in Being and Time.

signature_quotes.text: prescribes that way of interpreting the world and being-in-the-world
signature_quotes.context: Describing how the 'anyone-self' shapes human experience.

anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Meaning is not generated by isolated individuals; the primary disclosure of being is inherently social and prescribed by the 'anyone-self' or community.

While Heidegger held a racist and anti-Semitic conception of the essence of the German people, he explicitly rejected the Nazi's purely biologistic conception of race in favor of a shared history, soil, language, and destiny.
